flashcards.q: What was the Mandal Commission?
flashcards.a: An inquiry commission (1979) appointed to identify Socially and Educationally Backward Classes; recommended 27% job reservation for these classes.

flashcards.q: When was the reservation policy announced?
flashcards.a: August 13, 1990, through Office Memorandum No. 36012/31/90-Est.

flashcards.q: What is an Office Memorandum in government?
flashcards.a: An official written communication issuing government decisions or policies through appropriate ministry or department.

flashcards.q: How did the Supreme Court modify the reservation policy in 1992?
flashcards.a: Court upheld reservations as constitutional but ordered exclusion of well-to-do backward class members, ensuring benefits reach truly disadvantaged.

flashcards.q: What does it mean that major policy decisions involve multiple institutions?
flashcards.a: No single institution decides alone; Commission studies, Parliament debates, parties include in manifestos, elections occur, Cabinet decides, bureaucrats implement, courts review.

flashcards.q: What are the four functions of Parliament?
flashcards.a: Making and changing laws; controlling government; controlling public money; serving as highest forum for public debate.

flashcards.q: What is a no-confidence motion?
flashcards.a: A parliamentary vote on whether government retains Parliament's confidence; if passed, government must resign.

flashcards.q: How many members are in Lok Sabha and how are they elected?
flashcards.a: 543 members, directly elected by people from constituencies through universal adult franchise.

flashcards.q: How many members are in Rajya Sabha and how are they selected?
flashcards.a: 250 members; 238 elected by state legislatures and 12 nominated by President.

flashcards.q: What is the term length for Lok Sabha members?
flashcards.a: Five years; Lok Sabha can be dissolved before five years if government loses majority.

flashcards.q: What is the term length for Rajya Sabha members?
flashcards.a: Six years; one-third retire every two years; not dissolved; permanent body.

flashcards.q: Why is Lok Sabha more powerful than Rajya Sabha?
flashcards.a: Lok Sabha members are directly elected; they control government (no-confidence power); they dominate money bills; larger size (543 vs 250).

flashcards.q: What is Rajya Sabha's special power regarding money bills?
flashcards.a: Rajya Sabha cannot reject money bills; can only delay them by 14 days.

flashcards.q: What happens when Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha disagree on an ordinary law?
flashcards.a: Both houses meet in joint session; decision is made there; Lok Sabha's larger size gives its position advantage.

flashcards.q: What is Question Hour in Parliament?
flashcards.a: Daily period when MPs ask ministers about government policies and decisions; important accountability mechanism.

flashcards.q: Who is the head of government in India?
flashcards.a: The Prime Minister, appointed by President but must have Lok Sabha majority support.

flashcards.q: How is the Prime Minister appointed?
flashcards.a: President appoints the leader of the majority party or coalition in Lok Sabha as Prime Minister.

flashcards.q: What are the key powers of the Prime Minister?
flashcards.a: Chairs Cabinet; coordinates departments; has final say in disagreements; supervises all ministries; distributes ministerial work; can dismiss ministers.

flashcards.q: What happens when the Prime Minister resigns?
flashcards.a: The entire ministry resigns; all ministers must resign with the PM.

flashcards.q: What is the Cabinet of Ministers?
flashcards.a: The council of about 25 senior ministers headed by PM who meet to take most important policy decisions.

flashcards.q: What is collective responsibility in Cabinet?
flashcards.a: All Cabinet ministers must publicly support all Cabinet decisions, even if they disagreed during deliberation.

flashcards.q: What happens if a minister cannot support a Cabinet decision?
flashcards.a: The minister must resign; they cannot remain in Cabinet and publicly criticize Cabinet decisions.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between political executive and permanent executive?
flashcards.a: Political executive (ministers) makes policy decisions and is elected; permanent executive (civil servants) implements decisions and is appointed.

flashcards.q: Why must elected ministers have final decision-making power over appointed bureaucrats?
flashcards.a: Because ministers are electorally accountable to voters; bureaucrats are appointed and not directly accountable to people.

flashcards.q: What is the Supreme Court?
flashcards.a: The highest court in the country; capable of hearing appeals from lower courts and reviewing constitutionality of laws and government actions.

flashcards.q: What is judicial review?
flashcards.a: The power of courts to examine whether laws or government actions violate the Constitution and declare them invalid if they do.

flashcards.q: Can courts strike down Parliament's laws?
flashcards.a: Yes; if a law violates the Constitution, courts can declare it invalid through judicial review.

flashcards.q: Can courts strike down government actions?
flashcards.a: Yes; if government action violates Constitution or citizens' rights, courts can declare it invalid and issue orders preventing the action.

flashcards.q: What is a writ issued by courts?
flashcards.a: A formal order directing government officials to act or stop acting in a particular way; used to enforce rights.

flashcards.q: What is Public Interest Litigation (PIL)?
flashcards.a: A legal mechanism allowing any citizen or organization to approach courts on behalf of public interest, not just individual interest.

flashcards.q: Who can file a PIL?
flashcards.a: Any citizen, organization, or group interested in protecting public interest; doesn't need to be directly affected by the issue.

flashcards.q: What issues have been addressed through PIL in India?
flashcards.a: Environmental pollution, worker rights, bonded labor, child labor, right to food, street children's welfare, and government accountability.

flashcards.q: How are judges appointed to the Supreme Court and High Courts?
flashcards.a: President appoints them on advice of PM, but in practice senior judges of Supreme Court select new judges; political executive has limited influence.

flashcards.q: Can judges be arbitrarily removed from office?
flashcards.a: No; removal requires impeachment passed by two-thirds of both Parliament houses; has never happened in Indian democracy.

flashcards.q: What protects judges from being pressured by the government?
flashcards.a: Security of tenure (cannot be transferred against will), fixed salaries from government treasury, and independence from government control.

flashcards.q: What is the civil service?
flashcards.a: The permanent bureaucracy appointed through competitive examination, responsible for day-to-day administration and implementation of government decisions.

flashcards.q: Who are civil servants?
flashcards.a: Appointed government officials including IAS/IPS officers, teachers in government schools, doctors in government hospitals, police officers, tax collectors.

flashcards.q: Why is continuity of civil service important in democracy?
flashcards.a: When governments change through elections, civil servants continue serving, ensuring basic administration doesn't collapse during political transitions.

flashcards.q: What is the highest administrator in a government ministry?
flashcards.a: The Secretary, a senior civil servant who provides technical advice to the minister while ministers make policy decisions.

flashcards.q: Why do bureaucrats have more expertise than ministers?
flashcards.a: Bureaucrats dedicate their careers to one subject area; ministers change portfolios and may be appointed to unfamiliar ministries.

flashcards.q: What is the problem with concentrating all power in one person?
flashcards.a: It leads to tyranny; no checks exist; decisions cannot be reviewed; minorities cannot be protected; accountability disappears.

flashcards.q: Why are institutional procedures and checks important despite being slow?
flashcards.a: They prevent tyranny by distributing power, allowing multiple perspectives to be heard, protecting minorities, and ensuring accountability.

flashcards.q: What is the purpose of having separate legislative, executive, and judicial branches?
flashcards.a: Separation of powers ensures no single branch becomes too powerful; each branch checks the others.

flashcards.q: What does it mean when Parliament controls the government?
flashcards.a: Government must have Parliament's support to take major decisions; if Parliament passes no-confidence motion, government must resign.

flashcards.q: Can the government function without Parliament's support?
flashcards.a: No; in parliamentary democracy, government requires Lok Sabha's confidence to remain in power.

flashcards.q: What is the role of media in institutional accountability?
flashcards.a: Media reports on government and institution actions, keeps public informed, and creates pressure for accountability.

flashcards.q: How do citizens ensure institutions remain accountable?
flashcards.a: By voting, participating in politics, protesting against injustice, demanding transparency, and remaining vigilant about power abuses.
